blocks.text: Owned product strategy for an enterprise authentication initiative that balanced long-term platform scalability with near-term customer adoption. After evaluating a newer identity provider approach, we chose to extend the existing legacy authentication foundation with targeted improvements rather than force customers through a high-friction migration. This pragmatic path unblocked 95% of affected enterprise customers and accelerated $1M+ in revenue delayed by authentication limitations.
Problem

Enterprise customers needed secure onboarding and access patterns that fit their operational realities, but the path to modernization was not straightforward. A newer identity provider solution offered a cleaner long-term architecture, but migrating customers too quickly risked slowing adoption, increasing implementation complexity, and delaying revenue.
A newer IdP-based approach was more scalable long term, but introduced migration friction for existing enterprise customers.
Reusing the legacy authentication foundation was faster for adoption, but required targeted improvements to support enterprise onboarding needs.
Customers needed smoother access and onboarding flows without being forced into a disruptive auth migration.
Sales and Customer Success needed a faster path to unblock revenue-delaying authentication issues.
Engineering needed a practical migration path that improved the current system without creating unnecessary one-off workarounds.
My Role
As Product Manager, I owned product strategy, customer discovery, requirements, stakeholder alignment, and rollout sequencing.

I partnered with Engineering, Security, Customer Success, Sales, and enterprise stakeholders to evaluate the tradeoff between a new scalable IdP approach and a lower-friction path that extended the existing authentication foundation. I helped prioritize the path that reduced customer friction, accelerated adoption, and preserved room for future platform modernization.
Solution

We chose a pragmatic auth strategy: reuse the existing legacy authentication foundation, add targeted improvements to reduce onboarding friction, and sequence delivery around the customers most blocked by authentication limitations.

items.title: Targeted auth flow improvements
items.description: Focused enhancements to existing authentication flows where they were creating onboarding blockers.

items.title: Reduced onboarding friction
items.description: Smoother access patterns for enterprise customers without forcing immediate IdP migration.

items.title: Sequenced rollout
items.description: Delivery prioritized around customers with the largest adoption blockers and revenue impact.

items.title: Preserved future optionality
items.description: Improvements were structured to keep a path open to a more scalable identity architecture later.

items.title: Cross-functional alignment
items.description: Engineering, Security, Sales, and Customer Success aligned on the same customer readiness path.

items.title: Enterprise compatibility
items.description: Support for existing customer access patterns and security requirements without one-off workarounds.

blocks.text: Execution and Stakeholder Management
Evaluated a newer scalable identity provider approach against customer migration cost, implementation complexity, and adoption risk.
Determined that extending the existing authentication foundation would unblock customers faster than forcing a new IdP migration.
Prioritized improvements that reduced onboarding friction while preserving a path to future identity modernization.
Sequenced rollout around customers with the largest adoption blockers and revenue impact.
Partnered with Security and Engineering to ensure improvements met enterprise requirements without increasing implementation burden.
Aligned Sales and Customer Success on the customer readiness path so blocked accounts could move forward faster.
Key Product Decisions / Tradeoffs

blocks.tradeoff: A newer IdP solution was cleaner long term, but would have created migration friction for existing customers.
blocks.outcome: Chose a lower-friction path that reused the legacy foundation and improved adoption speed.

blocks.tradeoff: Avoided over-rotating toward the ideal architecture when the urgent business problem was blocked onboarding.
blocks.outcome: Unblocked 95% of affected customers and accelerated $1M+ in revenue.

blocks.tradeoff: Improved the current auth foundation instead of forcing a disruptive migration.
blocks.outcome: Reduced customer implementation burden while preserving future modernization optionality.

blocks.tradeoff: Balanced scalable identity patterns with compatibility for existing customer workflows.
blocks.outcome: Reduced one-off friction while supporting broader enterprise adoption.

blocks.tradeoff: Sequenced improvements based on customer impact, revenue blockers, and implementation effort.
blocks.outcome: Focused engineering effort on the highest-leverage adoption blockers first.

blocks.text: Results & Impact
Unblocked 95% of affected enterprise customers.
Accelerated $1M+ in revenue delayed by authentication limitations.
Reduced onboarding friction by improving the existing authentication path instead of forcing a disruptive IdP migration.
Improved GTM confidence by giving Sales and Customer Success a clearer path to unblock affected accounts.
Preserved future optionality for a more scalable identity architecture while solving the immediate adoption problem.
Created a pragmatic enterprise auth strategy grounded in customer adoption, not just technical modernization.
Metrics Tracked
Percentage of affected customers unblocked
Revenue accelerated
Customer onboarding completion rate
Time to onboard affected enterprise accounts
Authentication-related deployment blockers
Customer migration friction
Adoption readiness by customer segment
Engineering effort vs customer impact
Key Learnings
The best platform decision is not always the cleanest technical architecture. Sometimes the right product strategy is the path customers can actually adopt.
Enterprise auth is both a technical system and an adoption/revenue lever.
Modernization needs to be sequenced around customer migration friction, not just internal architecture goals.
In enterprise healthcare, reducing implementation burden can matter as much as adding new platform capabilities.
Strong PM judgment means balancing future-state scalability with near-term customer activation.

blocks.note: Selected path: Option B. Preserves future optionality to revisit IdP modernization once the immediate adoption blockers are resolved.
